title: Culture-Aware Humorous Captioning: Multimodal Humor Generation across Cultural Contexts
research_questions: Can multimodal large language models generate short humorous captions that remain grounded in an input image while adapting to an explicitly specified cultural context?
How can open-ended, culture-conditioned humorous image captions be evaluated across image grounding, cultural appropriateness, semantic quality, coherence, humor, and creativity?

Does a staged alignment framework combining Western-context supervised fine-tuning, judge-based GRPO, degradation-aware regularization, and low-resource Eastern adaptation improve contextual fit and overall caption quality?
Does explicit cultural context conditioning improve performance compared with no cultural context conditioning?

datasets_used.name: culture-aware humorous captioning training pool
datasets_used.size: 3,500 images; Stage 1 uses 3,000 image-text samples; Stage 3 uses 1,000 image-text pairs

methods_proposed.name: CuHAlign
methods_proposed.summary: A three-stage alignment framework for culture-aware humorous captioning. It first performs Western-context supervised fine-tuning on Qwen3-VL-8B with LoRA, then applies judge-based GRPO with multi-dimensional preference rankings, and finally adapts to Eastern cultural context using a small supervised mixture with Western replay.

methods_proposed.name: Degradation-aware Prototype Repulsion Constraint
methods_proposed.summary: A constraint added during preference optimization to mitigate reward hacking in open-ended generation. It builds prototype vectors for degradation directions from annotated evidence texts and penalizes generated outputs whose representation is too similar to the corresponding degradation prototype.

methods_proposed.name: Six-dimensional evaluation framework
methods_proposed.summary: An evaluation rubric scoring Image Relevance, Contextual Fit, Semantic Richness, Reasonableness, Humor, and Creativity on 10-point scales. The paper reports each dimension and their unweighted average.

evaluation_methods.detail: A fixed 20% subset of the 1,000 test samples is scored by human annotators using the six-dimensional 10-point rubric.

The remaining 80% of the 1,000 test samples is scored automatically by an LLM-as-a-Judge under the same unified rubric, and final results average human-scored and judge-scored portions over the full test set.

For preference alignment, the current policy samples K = 8 rollouts per training sample; the rollouts plus a reference caption are ranked by an LLM judge, and rewards are constructed from each rollout's rank relative to the reference.
A judge validation set of 400 image instances is built as image + high-quality caption + low-quality caption pairs with manually predefined better-worse labels.
GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, and Claude Sonnet 4.5 are evaluated as pairwise judges on the 400-instance validation set; GPT-5.1 has the highest agreement in all four language/difficulty settings.

headline_results.finding: CuHAlign achieved the highest Contextual Fit under the Western cultural context, improving over the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct backbone from 5.49 to 6.63.
CuHAlign achieved the highest Contextual Fit under the Eastern cultural context, improving over the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct backbone from 5.22 to 6.30.
On overall average in Table 2, GPT-4o scored 6.76 under Western context and 6.75 under Eastern context, compared with CuHAlign's 6.63 and 6.66.
Explicit cultural conditioning increased CuHAlign's Contextual Fit from 5.21 with no context to 6.63 under Western context and 6.30 under Eastern context.
In the ablation, Western-context CF rose from 5.49 for the base model to 6.13 after SFT, 6.59 after GRPO, 6.62 after the degradation constraint, and 6.63 for the full model.
GPT-5.1 had the highest judge-human agreement across all judge-validation settings: 68.2, 77.3, 71.6, and 79.2.

key_findings: Contextual Fit separates culture-aware humorous captioning from general humorous captioning: model gaps are larger on CF than on several general caption-quality dimensions.
CuHAlign obtains the best CF under both cultural settings while maintaining competitive overall scores, but GPT-4o has the highest overall averages in Table 2.
Explicit cultural context substantially improves CF for both Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct and CuHAlign compared with the no-context setting.

The staged alignment ablation shows SFT establishes task behavior, GRPO is the main step for improving generation quality, the degradation-aware constraint adds a smaller balance improvement, and Eastern adaptation is crucial for the low-resource Eastern setting.
Judge validation shows automatic evaluation is more reliable for clear quality gaps and harder for fine-grained ambiguous pairs; GPT-5.1 performs best among the three judges tested.
limitations: The paper states that the Chinese–Eastern and English–Western setup may not fully disentangle language effects from cultural effects.
The task uses only two coarse-grained cultural conditions, Eastern and Western, and the paper states this is not a fine-grained or exhaustive definition of culture.
Benchmark references are intended as stable high-quality anchors and are not intended to exhaust all valid humorous expressions.
Automatic judge evaluation is reliable for obvious quality gaps but remains challenging for fine-grained discrimination on ambiguous pairs.

human_samples.population: Human annotators; expertise, cultural background, and demographics not reported.
human_samples.observations: A fixed 20% subset of the 1,000 test samples was scored by human annotators using the six 10-point dimensions; the remaining 80% was scored by an LLM-as-a-Judge and final metrics were averaged over the full test set.
human_samples.detail: Section 7.1 states a human-machine hybrid protocol. It does not report the number of annotators, ratings per item, adjudication, compensation, or inter-annotator agreement. Western-context inference/evaluation/output used English; Eastern-context inference/evaluation/output used Chinese.

human_samples.population: Humans involved in data quality control; identities and expertise not reported.
human_samples.observations: High-quality captioned data were constructed with candidate generation, automatic screening, manual spot-checking, and additional review of ambiguous cases when necessary.
human_samples.detail: Section 5 describes manual spot-checking for training supervision and benchmark reference construction, but gives no annotator count, sampling rate for spot checks, or detailed annotation protocol. Section 7.5 calls the high-quality captions used in judge validation 'human reference captions,' while Section 5 describes a generated-candidate-plus-screening pipeline; human authorship/count is therefore not fully specified.

human_samples.population: Humans who manually predefined better-worse labels for judge validation; no further details reported.
human_samples.observations: 400 image instances were used for pairwise judge validation: image + high-quality caption + low-quality caption, with manually predefined better-worse labels. The first 200 cases had relatively large quality gaps and the remaining 200 had smaller, more ambiguous differences.
human_samples.detail: These labels served as the reference for computing agreement of GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, and Claude Sonnet 4.5 in Figure 3.

judges.detail: Human annotators scored the fixed 20% test subset on Image Relevance, Contextual Fit, Semantic Richness, Reasonableness, Humor, and Creativity. Expertise/blinding were not reported.

An LLM-as-a-Judge scored the remaining 80% of test samples under the same six-dimensional rubric. The paper validates GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, and Claude Sonnet 4.5 and says GPT-5.1 performs best, but Section 7.1 does not explicitly name the final absolute-score judge.

For GRPO preference alignment, an LLM judge ranked K=8 sampled rollouts plus the reference caption for each training sample; rewards were constructed from each rollout's rank relative to the reference. The judge model name and prompt details are not reported in the supplied text.
Manually predefined better-worse labels on the 400-instance judge validation set were used as the reference labels for LLM-judge agreement.

judges.name: GPT-5.1
judges.detail: Evaluated as a pairwise judge on the 400-instance validation set. Reported agreement with manual labels: 68.2, 77.3, 71.6, and 79.2 across the four language/difficulty settings shown in Figure 3.

judges.name: Gemini-3-Flash-Preview
judges.detail: Evaluated as a pairwise judge on the 400-instance validation set. Reported agreement: 63.7, 71.9, 65.1, and 77.6 across the four settings.

judges.name: Claude Sonnet 4.5
judges.detail: Evaluated as a pairwise judge on the 400-instance validation set. Reported agreement: 61.8, 69.5, 67.3, and 75.4 across the four settings.
Automatic screening was part of the caption-data construction pipeline and helped determine retained captions; the screening model/metric and thresholds are not specified.
Manual spot-checking and ambiguous-case review were part of data construction. Annotator details and decision rules are not reported.

generation_selection.scope: Benchmark/test-time caption generation for Tables 2, 3, and 4
generation_selection.detail: Models were required to generate a short humorous caption for the input image and cultural-context condition, but the paper does not state whether outputs were single-sample, greedy, or selected from multiple candidates; no decoding or post-processing procedure is reported.

For standard caption samples, candidate captions were generated for each image-context pair and one caption was retained after screening. The number of candidates, generation models, and exact selector composition are not reported; the pipeline included automatic screening, manual spot-checking, and review of ambiguous cases.

generation_selection.n: K = 8 sampled rollouts plus one reference caption per training sample
generation_selection.detail: The K rollouts were not reported as a best-of-N inference output. They were jointly ranked by an LLM judge against a reference caption, and all sampled rollouts received rank-derived rewards for GRPO updates.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Used as a general-purpose multimodal baseline in Table 2. No API snapshot, inference date, access route, or decoding setup is reported.

model_provenance.name: gemini-3-flash-preview
model_provenance.detail: Used as a Table 2 generation baseline and also represented in judge validation as Gemini-3-Flash-Preview. Reference [68] is official Gemini API documentation accessed 2026-04-02, but this is not reported as the inference date.

model_provenance.name: Claude Sonnet 4.5_pred
model_provenance.detail: Used as a Table 2 generation baseline under this exact row name. No inference date, API snapshot, or decoding setup is reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude Sonnet 4.5
model_provenance.detail: Used as a pairwise judge in Figure 3. Reference [69] is the Claude Sonnet 4.5 system card accessed 2026-04-02; this is not reported as the inference date.

model_provenance.name: InternVL3-8B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Used as a baseline in Tables 2 and 3. Checkpoint/source, inference date, access route, and decoding parameters are not reported.

model_provenance.name: LLaVA-OneVision-7B
model_provenance.detail: Used as a Table 2 baseline. No checkpoint hash, inference date, access route, or decoding parameters are reported.

model_provenance.name: MiniCPM-V 2.6
model_provenance.detail: Used as a baseline in Tables 2 and 3. No inference-date or decoding details are reported.

model_provenance.name: MiniGPT-4
model_provenance.detail: Used as a Table 2 baseline. No checkpoint or inference details are reported.

model_provenance.name: GLM-4V-9B
model_provenance.detail: Used as a Table 2 baseline. No checkpoint, access, or decoding details are reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Used as a Table 2 baseline. No checkpoint hash or inference details are reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Used as the backbone baseline in Tables 2, 3, and 4. No checkpoint hash, inference date, or decoding setup is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-8B
model_provenance.detail: Adopted as the CuHAlign backbone and fine-tuned with LoRA. The paper alternates between Qwen3-VL-8B in the method section and Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct in the baseline tables; exact checkpoint provenance is not reported.

model_provenance.name: cogvlm2-llama3-chat-19B
model_provenance.detail: Used as a Table 2 baseline. No checkpoint hash, inference date, or decoding setup is reported.

model_provenance.name: QwenVL+CLoT
model_provenance.detail: Used as the prior relevant method baseline in Table 2. Exact implementation/checkpoint and inference settings are not reported.

model_provenance.name: CuHAlign
model_provenance.detail: Authors' trained model, based on Qwen3-VL-8B with LoRA and three stages: Western SFT, judge-based GRPO with degradation-aware constraint, and Eastern adaptation. No released checkpoint, training compute, or inference-date details are reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-5.1
model_provenance.detail: Run as a pairwise LLM judge in Figure 3; likely selected for judge use based on the reported validation result, but the supplied text does not give an API snapshot, access route, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: unnamed LLM-as-a-Judge
model_provenance.detail: The main 80% automatic scoring judge and the GRPO ranking judge are described functionally; the supplied text does not unambiguously name the final absolute-score judge or the GRPO judge.

model_provenance.name: unnamed candidate-generation model(s) for data construction
model_provenance.detail: High-quality captioned data were built from generated candidates followed by screening; the paper says subsets differ partly in the model used for candidate generation, but does not name those models.

model_provenance.name: unnamed unified text encoder
model_provenance.detail: Used to map degradation-evidence texts and generated outputs into a shared representation space for the Degradation-aware Prototype Repulsion Constraint. The encoder identity is not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Main benchmark caption generation under Western and Eastern cultural contexts
inference_budgets.detail: Benchmark contains 1,000 images. Models were evaluated on the same test split, separately under Western and Eastern contexts. Western setting used English prompts/evaluation/outputs; Eastern setting used Chinese prompts/evaluation/outputs. Calls per item, candidate counts, decoding parameters, token limits, and cost are not reported.

InternVL3-8B-Instruct, MiniCPM-V 2.6, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, and CuHAlign were compared on the same 1,000 test samples under no context, Western context, and Eastern context. Generation budget and decoding settings are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Hybrid evaluation scoring for main results
inference_budgets.detail: A fixed 20% subset was scored by human annotators and the remaining 80% by an LLM-as-a-Judge using the six-dimensional 10-point rubric. The number of LLM calls, score aggregation details, LLM sampling settings, and human ratings per item are not reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 2 captions per image instance: one high-quality caption and one low-quality caption
inference_budgets.detail: 400 image instances from the test set were used. GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, and Claude Sonnet 4.5 performed pairwise judgments. The paper does not report prompt token limits, repetition, sampling parameters, or cost.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: K = 8 sampled rollouts plus 1 reference caption per training sample
inference_budgets.detail: The LLM judge outputs a joint ranking over the candidate set; rewards are rank-relative to the reference. The exact number of judge calls per training sample, rollout decoding settings, reward mapping f, and judge prompt are not reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: plural candidate captions per image-context pair; one retained after screening; number not reported
inference_budgets.detail: The same construction pipeline of candidate generation, automatic screening, manual spot-checking, and ambiguous-case review was applied to training supervision and benchmark reference construction. Candidate-generation model names, number of candidates, prompts, decoding settings, screening thresholds, and cost are not reported.

Stage 1 uses 3,000 Western-context image-text samples for SFT. Stage 3 uses 1,000 image-text pairs: 50% new Eastern-context images, 30% Eastern captions for images already in the training pool, and 20% Western replay. LoRA is used, but optimizer, epochs, batch size, hardware, training time, and cost are not reported.

human_baselines.description: No human caption-generation system or human-authored outputs are reported as a competing baseline scored under the same six-dimensional evaluation protocol. Reference captions and manually predefined labels are used as anchors/validation targets, not as a directly comparable human performance baseline.
human_baselines.detail: Tables 2-4 compare MLLMs, the Qwen3-VL backbone, QwenVL+CLoT, and CuHAlign; no row reports human generator performance.

mitigations: The constructed train, development, and benchmark splits are image-disjoint within the authors' 5,000-image filtered pool.
The benchmark references are newly constructed under both Western and Eastern contexts rather than copied as a conventional captioning benchmark in the supplied text.
All compared models are evaluated on the same test split.
contamination_risk.basis: The paper introduces a new culture-aware benchmark, but its raw images are collected from CLoT, a prior public dataset, and no decontamination test or model-training-data audit is reported. Several evaluated proprietary and open models may have been trained after CLoT or on web/image-caption data, and exact inference dates/snapshots are missing. The image-disjoint split controls leakage from the authors' fine-tuning data into their benchmark, but it does not rule out pretraining or prior-benchmark exposure for evaluated external models.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Main model comparisons in Table 2, the explicit-context results in Table 3, ablation conclusions in Table 4, and CuHAlign's preference-alignment training all rely substantially on LLM judgments; 80% of the main evaluation set is LLM-scored and GRPO rewards are generated by an LLM judge.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: The paper uses human scoring for a fixed 20% test subset and constructs a 400-instance pairwise judge validation set with manually predefined better-worse labels. GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, and Claude Sonnet 4.5 are compared against those labels, with GPT-5.1 highest in all four reported settings.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: Because headline quantitative results are computed from a hybrid protocol dominated by LLM-scored items, and because the proposed method's preference-alignment stage is judge-based, LLM judging is a primary determinant of both evaluation outcomes and the trained method. Human validation is present but limited and not reported with annotator counts or inter-annotator agreement.

reporting_gaps: Number of human annotators, recruitment, demographics, cultural/language background, expertise, compensation, and geography are not reported.
Number of human ratings per item, aggregation method, adjudication, inter-annotator agreement, and blinding/randomization for human evaluation are not reported.
The exact fixed 20% human-scored subset selection procedure is not described, and separate human-only versus LLM-only scores are not reported.
The final LLM used for the 80% absolute-score evaluation is not unambiguously named in Section 7.1; the GRPO ranking judge is also unnamed.
LLM judge prompts, scoring calibration procedure, temperature/top-p/max-token settings, repetition, and API snapshots/inference dates are not reported.

Test-time caption-generation budgets for all evaluated models are missing: calls per item, number of candidates, decoding parameters, token limits, post-processing, and whether any best-of-N selection was used.
Evaluated model provenance is incomplete: checkpoint hashes, API model snapshots, access routes, inference dates, and implementation details are not reported for most baselines.

Data-construction details are incomplete: candidate-generation models, number of generated candidates per image-context pair, automatic screening criteria/thresholds, manual spot-check rate, annotator counts, and ambiguous-case resolution process are not reported.
Training details for CuHAlign are incomplete: LoRA hyperparameters, optimizer, learning rates, batch sizes, epochs, hardware, training time, and compute/cost are not reported.

GRPO details are incomplete: exact reward mapping function f, relative-advantage transformation, number of training samples used in Stage 2, rollout decoding settings, and LLM judge call structure are not reported.

Degradation-aware constraint details are incomplete: degradation annotation size/process, text encoder identity, prototype construction details beyond averaging, threshold m, and lambda are not reported.
No decontamination analysis is reported for using CLoT-derived images with later proprietary or open models.
Artifact availability is not reported in the supplied text: no code URL, data URL, prompts, or model checkpoint link is provided.
The paper acknowledges that the Chinese–Eastern and English–Western setup may not disentangle language effects from cultural effects, and it only instantiates two coarse-grained cultural conditions.
